description: Fun counting and numbers learning song with repetitive structure for memorization
suno_tags: cheerful children's pop, bright female vocals, piano, hand claps, xylophone, bouncy, 95 BPM
vocal_style: Clear bright adult female voice, enthusiastic and encouraging, with deliberate pacing on numbers for learning

tips: Count clearly — numbers should fall on strong beats
Each verse covers a set of numbers (1-3, 4-6, 7-10)
Pair each number with a concrete object kids can picture
Use fingers and body parts for physical engagement
Chorus reinforces the joy of counting, not the numbers themselves

example_lyrics:
[Intro]
[Instrumental]

[Verse 1]
One little sun up in the sky
Two little birds that fly so high
Three little fish swim in the sea
Count along now, one two three!

[Chorus]
Let's count together, me and you
Counting's fun, there's so much to do
One two three four five and more
Let's see what numbers have in store!

[Verse 2]
Four little flowers in a row
Five little seeds we plant and grow
Six little bees buzz all around
Seven little worms beneath the ground

[Chorus]
Let's count together, me and you
Counting's fun, there's so much to do
One two three four five and more
Let's see what numbers have in store!

[Verse 3]
Eight little stars that shine at night
Nine little candles burning bright
Ten little fingers, wave them high
We counted to ten — oh my oh my!

[Chorus]
Let's count together, me and you
Counting's fun, there's so much to do
One two three four five and more
We counted to ten — let's count once more!

[Outro]
One, two, three, four, five, six, seven, eight, nine, ten!
[End]
do: Place numbers on strong beats so they stand out clearly
Use concrete nouns kids can visualize with each number
Make the final chorus slightly different to mark completion
Include a counting recap in the outro
dont: Never use children's choir or kids singing in suno_tags
Don't skip numbers or count out of order (confuses learners)
Avoid abstract concepts — keep everything concrete and visual
Don't go beyond 10 for this age group
Avoid rushing through numbers — pacing matters for learning
